1910–2007, lived and worked in Riga, Latvia
Auseklis Baušķenieks
(1910–2007) was an artist, painter, teacher, radio engineer, and amateur physicist. He studied at the Faculty of Architecture at the University of Latvia and at the Art Academy of Latvia. He also led a drawing and sculpture class at the Palace of Young Pioneers in Riga.

name: Nell BrookfieldBritish,b. 1994
bio:
Nell Brookfield
British,
b. 1994
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b. 1994, London, UK, lives and works in London
Nell puts her acute anthropologist’s eye to use in her work. She distills her observations into arresting images at once familiar and unsettling. They offer us an enhanced mirror, reflecting not only what can be seen, but also what is unseen, unspoken, hidden and repressed. Reality, but as seen in dreams and visions. She uses figures of animals as well as humans, not least to emphasise the animal in all of us. She mixes her own pigments to ensure the depth of colour, and texture, she requires, paying close attention to bodies, gestures, fabrics, hair, feathers, scales, fur and skin.

name: Justin Caguiatb. 1989
bio:
Justin Caguiat
b. 1989
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1989, Tokyo, Japan, lives and works in NY and Oakland
Justin Caguiat’s numinous, dreamlike paintings depict realms beyond the visible. Figures and other lifeforms stir and recede into aqueous grounds of patterned color, which appear to overspill the bounds of unstretched sheets of linen or canvas. His work’s layered atmospherics reflect the Symbolist inheritance of fin-de-siècle eccentrics like Odilon Redon, who also turned to sources of enchantment and mystery in an era of rampant capitalism. Though unmistakably contemporary, Caguiat’s paintings draw on a mix of aesthetic traditions, from Vuillard’s wallpapered interiors to the Vienna School’s jewel-box modernism, to the flattened forms of Japanese woodblock prints, mosaics and Manga imagery. Ornate but never ornamental, his work creates its own reality, through inner landscapes that drift between the observed and the imagined.

name: Joseph ChoïKorean,b. 1968
bio:
Joseph Choï
Korean,
b. 1968
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b. 1968, South Korea, lives and works in Paris
The first impression of Choi’s paintings is often surreal or dreamlike. However, rather than depicting fantasy, they feature real-looking figures, objects, and spaces arranged in a way that does not exist in reality. Every painting includes at least one figure—sometimes alone, sometimes in pairs, or occasionally in larger groups. While their identities remain ambiguous, similar facial features appear repeatedly. Most of his figures are set against interior spaces. Depending on the number of figures, the spatial background, and the emphasis on certain aspects of the figures, his works can be categorized into distinct series.

b.1974 in Ivory Coast. Lives and works  in Abidjan, Ivory Coast
Joana Choumali works mainly on conceptual portraiture, mixed media and documentary photography. Much of her work focuses on what she learns about the countless cultures around her. In her latest works, she embroiders directly on the images completing the act of creating the photographic image with a slow and meditative gesture.

name: Parmen DaushviliGeorgian,b. 1970
bio:
Parmen Daushvili
Georgian,
b. 1970
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b. 1970, Tbilisi, Georgia, lives and works in London
Parmen Daushvili’s paintings are survivors of their own making - they are over-painted, scraped, sanded, re-painted and transformed. Daushvili began painting in 2003, but it wasn’t until after fleeing Tbilisi for the United Kingdom that he became a full time painter, focusing initially on portrait commissions he received via social media.
He now finds himself inspired by his immediate surroundings, his friends and family, the light that seeps into his Kensington studio or the passerbyers he views from his window. Daushvili’s compositions are often subjected to a rigorous process of reduction, stripping them down to their essence until only the faintest traces remain. His brushwork is economical and his palette somber and measured. The resulting paintings radiate a sense of displacement, otherness and longing.

name: Rafael DelacruzAmerican,b. 1989
bio:
Rafael Delacruz
American,
b. 1989
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1989 in San Francisco, California, lives and works in Berkeley and New York
Rafael Delacruz’s paintings blend absurdity with satire. His work chronicles interactions with urban architecture in dystopian landscapes, places where figures blend into street corners and objects transform into body parts. Delacruz distorts and morphs images for his paintings, first with a computer and then with the paintbrush.

name: Alexander DikKazakh,b. 1983
bio:
Alexander Dik
Kazakh,
b. 1983
Overview
Biography
Exhibition View
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1983, Lenger, Kazakhstan, lives and works in Berlin
His neo-expressionist and symbolic works, raw and unfiltered, echo Berlin’s
contradictions—violence, displacement, and resilience. Working in a
countryside studio, Dik channels his ideas onto multiple canvases at once, often
incorporating the textures of his environment into his "floor paintings." His
techniques range from Pollock-like splashes to layered, mountainous textures,
pushing the boundaries of oil paint.
Dik’s art confronts grand themes of origin, war, and displacement, often
provocative and unsettling, yet deeply resonant. Inspired by artists like Georg
Baselitz, he embraces controversy, challenging norms both as a man and a
painter.

name: Willehad EilersGerman,b. 1980
bio:
Willehad Eilers
German,
b. 1980
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1981 in Peine, Germany, lives and works in Amsterdam, Netherlands
Willehad Eilers, also known as Wayne Horse, has been thematically concerned for some time with the ambivalent wealth of Western society - the "wallowing of pigs in paradise", as Eilers says. "The wealth and material abundance of people today leave traces in their self-image". When there are no serious problems, when there is nothing left to wish for, it can’t get worse. What is left is the very own self-optimization and self-realization. But the range of what is offered as success or as a 'fulfilled life' is limited.
By looking at social media the bitter taste of the feeling that everyone enjoys a continual, everlasting vacation - a reckless moment – remains. Certainly, the viewer may approach the artist's scenes with humor and irony, for the cheerful exaggeration marks an elementary aspect of his works of art in order to break the spell between viewer and object and to initiate a dialogue.
View artist website

name: Zohar FraimannIsraeli,b. 1987
bio:
Zohar Fraimann
Israeli,
b. 1987
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1987 in Jerusalem, Israel, lives and works in Berlin
Zohar Fraiman is an artist who examines the influence of digitalization on gender identity and our lives in general as reflected in our obsession with social media and our addiction to smartphones. Her work explores how identities are formed and through which mechanisms they are reconstructed in digital spaces such as Instagram, Tinder and Tik Tok. With an abundance of humour, bright colours and fractured characters that bear reference to Disney films, retro advertising, iconic pop culture and the works of old masters, Fraiman questions the practice of internet-based self-staging and criticizes exaggerated and distorted ways of expressing both gender and the self within image- based networks and social platforms.

b.1980, Boston, USA, lives and works in Brooklyn, NY
Daniel Gordon holds a BA from Bard College and an MFA from Yale School of Art. Recent solo exhibitions include:
Free Transform
, Kasmin, New York, NY (2023),
Green Apples and Boots
, Huxley-Parlour, London, UK (2021),
Blue Room
, James Fuentes, New York (2018),
Selective Color
, M+B, Los Angeles (2017),
New Canvas
, James Fuentes, New York (2017),
Hand, Select & Invert Layer
, Bolte Lang, Zürich (2016), Switzerland;
Shadows, Patterns, Pears
, Foam Museum, Amsterdam, NL (2014); and
Screen Selections and Still Lifes
, Wallspace, New York, NY (2014). He has participated in several museum group exhibitions, including Cut! Paper Play in Contemporary Photography, J. Paul Getty Museum, Los Angeles, CA (2018), Secondhand, Pier 24, San Francisco, CA (2016) Greater New York, MoMA P.S. 1, Queens, NY (2010) New Photography Series, Museum of Modern Art, New York, NY (2009).

name: Peter HerrmannGerman,b. 1937
bio:
Peter Herrmann
German,
b. 1937
Overview
Biography
Works
View works.
Exhibition View
View works
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1937 Zittau, Germany, lives and works in Berlin
Peter Herrmann, one of the greatest artists of his generation, witnessed the division of East and West Germany. His artistic style, reminiscent of early Ernst Ludwig Kirchner and Henri Rousseau, carries a playful spirit akin to the Junge Wilde movement while maintaining its own distinct voice. Deeply devoted to painting, he approaches his craft with both seriousness and ease, skillfully intertwining different moments and eras. His goal is not merely to be modern; the luminous colors of Piero della Francesca and the enigmatic shadows of Arnold Böcklin resonate in his work, lending it a timeless presence. Frequently described as "dream paintings," his art bridges history and the present, offering viewers a sense of continuity and connection across time and culture.

name: Jaeyoung KangSouth Korean
bio:
Jaeyoung Kang
South Korean
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b. 198?, South Korea, lives and works in South Korea
Jaeyoung Kang expresses the complex and entangled phenomena surrounding him through an intuitive visual language, relying on the flow of the subconscious.
Kang’s paintings reflect his sense of dissonance and temporal displacement in navigating societal norms and adulthood. These complexities manifest in his work through distorted expressions, leaps of imagination, and unconventional materials. His process is fast-paced and gestural, emphasizing the act of painting itself. He employs rough brushstrokes, knife-scraping, layering diverse materials, smearing paint with his hands, and using spray techniques to create drips and fluid marks.

name: Hyungdae KimSouth Korean,b. 1936
bio:
Hyungdae Kim
South Korean,
b. 1936
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1936, South Korea, lives and works in South Korea
Hyungdae Kim is a pioneering South Korean artist whose work bridges the traditional and modern, blending organic forms and geometric abstraction. Known for his mastery in woodcut printmaking, Kim’s art evolves from intense gestural expression to intricate explorations of materiality, color, and texture. His works resonate with the rhythms of nature and the cultural heritage of Korea, while simultaneously pushing the boundaries of abstraction.

name: Keuntai KimSouth Korean,b. 1953
bio:
Keuntai Kim
South Korean,
b. 1953
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b. 1953, South Korea, works and lives in South Korea
Keuntai Kim explores the nature of perception, emphasizing that seeing extends beyond physical vision. They challenge the notion that we perceive purely through our eyes, proposing that perception is shaped by memory, experience, and accumulated information.
He believes in the potential for genuine self-reflective seeing through questioning and introspection. The concept of 'self-examination' is integral to their practice, encouraging both artist and viewer to strip away conditioned perceptions.
Ultimately, their work is an ongoing inquiry that invites contemplation rather than providing definitive answers, challenging us to reconsider what and how we see.

name: Yunyoung KimSouth Korean,b. 1989
bio:
Yunyoung Kim
South Korean,
b. 1989
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1989 in Gangneung, South Korea, lives and works in Halle
In her work, the body serves as more than just a physical entity that constitutes the self; it acts as an essential medium through which one perceives and communicates with others and the world. The body is a key tool for understanding space, recognizing objects, and forming meaning through physical experiences in the material world.
Through the process of weaving thread by thread, the artist explores the relationship between bodies and illustrates the interconnectedness between the self and the other.

name: Shine KongSouth Korean,b. 1979
bio:
Shine Kong
South Korean,
b. 1979
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1979 in Gangneung, South Korea, lives and works in Gangneung, South Korea
Shine Kong’s paintings possess a tactile quality—small yet structurally intricate. Her brushstrokes are never excessive or lacking; they maintain a sense of order and composure, reflecting the fact that they follow a world her hands have already touched. The dual process of physical embodiment and painterly representation prevents her work from veering into abstraction or uncontrolled emotional turbulence. Every piece undergoes a form of tactile curation, a preemptive balance of structure and sensation.
The scale of Shine Kong’s paintings aligns precisely with this manual craftsmanship. Her sense of space and texture emerges from this approach, and her brushwork moves within the comfort of familiarity, much like a traveler navigating a well-known landscape. This is the key to the warmth and humanity in her work. It stands in stark contrast to the often cold, detached precision of photorealists who replicate photographic images.

name: Paulo NazarethBrazilian,b. 1977
bio:
Paulo Nazareth
Brazilian,
b. 1977
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1977, Governador Valadares, Brasil, lives and works in Minas Gerais, Brasil  (when he is not travelling)
Living as a global nomad, Paulo Nazareth’s work is often the result of precise and simple gestures, which bring about broader ramifications, raising awareness to press issues of immigration, racialization, globalization, colonialism, and its effects in the production and consumption of art in his native Brazil and the Global South. While his work may manifest in video, photography, and found objects, his strongest medium may be cultivating relationships with people he encounters on the road — particularly those who must remain invisible due to their legal status or those who are repressed by governmental authorities. In certain aspects, Nazareth deliberately embodies the romantic ideal of the wandering artist in search of himself and universal truths, to unveil stereotyped assumptions about national identity, cultural history, and human value.

name: Pierre SeinturierFrench,b. 1988
bio:
Pierre Seinturier
French,
b. 1988
Overview
Biography
Installation shots
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1988 in Paris, France, lives and works in Paris
Pierre Seinturier is known for his distinctive blend of visual storytelling, dry wit, and noir-inspired cinematic references. His works create an atmosphere of suspense, where the familiar turns unsettling, and the ordinary hides layers of intrigue.
In imagined dialogue, the artist explores the role of the creator and his own relationship with the act of making art. His paintings and drawings unfold as narrative fragments—scenes filled with mystery, humor, and the lingering tension of something just about to happen.
With his unique talent, sharp wit, and inventive approach, Seinturier invites us into his world, challenging our perceptions and encouraging us to question the boundaries between fiction and reality.

b.1980 in South Korea, lives and works in Seoul, South Korea
Gee Song's work is characterised by the superimposition and rearrangement of images of everyday objects, architecture and nature, making them appear surreal and artificial. The flat, perspective-less canvases are constructed from the artist's imagination, inviting the viewer to experience a fictionalised reality.

name: Gyaeyoung SongSouth Korean,b. 1965
bio:
Gyaeyoung Song
South Korean,
b. 1965
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1965 in South Korea, lives and works in Daejeon, South Korea
Paper is the primary material in Song’s work, and she views it as more than just a medium—it becomes an organism that embodies the processes of growth, decay, and evolution. Her work extends into the realms of biotechnology and genetic manipulation, driven by a curiosity about the mutations and new life forms that could emerge from such scientific advancements. The transformed forms in her pieces serve as metaphors for the progress of life sciences that challenge the natural order, while also addressing the extinction and mutations caused by environmental destruction. Through these visual representations, Song raises profound questions about the essence and identity of life, creating new life forms that transcend species boundaries.

name: Nick TaggartEnglish,b. 1954
bio:
Nick Taggart
English,
b. 1954
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
b.1954, Stockport, England, lives and works in Los Angeles, CA, US
Taggart’s work captures the essence of 1970s and ‘80s Los Angeles. His portraits of models, musicians, and actors immerse viewers in the era’s unique atmosphere, reflecting not just fashion and lifestyle but also architecture, design, commercial culture and street life. Other works document LA’s iconic locations, from the Sunset Strip to unassuming big-box stores, creating a vivid tableau where past and present collide. Straddling the line between idealized and documentary, Taggart’s work offers a rich, transportive glimpse into a nowvanished era of the city.

German,
1976-2020
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
1976-2020, Berlin, Germany
Excerpts and details of real places form a central point of reference in Weissbach's oeuvre. In the isolation of these places from their original context, in their appropriation and transformation into new contexts of meaning, worldly conditions and limitations shift and create layers of time between the present and past.

name: Miran YoonSouth Korean,b. 1948
bio:
Miran Yoon
South Korean,
b. 1948
Overview
Biography
Share
Facebook
X
Pinterest
Tumblr
Email
Miran Yoon’s work explores the delicate balance between fragility and boldness, using Hanji as a key medium. Her process of layering and tearing the paper reflects a tension between softness and rupture, revealing both calmness and intensity.
Yoon’s work transcends mere technique, creating a dialogue between beauty and tension, softness and aggression, where even the most fragile elements become powerful expressions of quiet rebellion. Through her art, she invites the viewer to witness the complexity of transformation within a seemingly simple medium.
